_comment: Curated explanatory notes for core concepts FIBO ships without one. Grounded in the concept's role in loan origination; applied by etl/to_okf.py when FIBO has no explanatoryNote, provenance curated.

notes.title: AmortizationType
notes.note: Determines how principal and interest are paid down over the term — fully amortizing loans retire the balance by maturity, while interest-only or balloon structures leave principal outstanding.

notes.title: Appraisal
notes.note: Ordered by the lender during underwriting to establish the collateral's value; it constrains the maximum loan amount through the loan-to-value ratio.

notes.title: AppraisedValue
notes.note: Serves as the denominator of the loan-to-value ratio and the basis for how much a lender will advance against the property.

notes.title: Borrower
notes.note: In residential origination the borrower is usually an individual or couple; the lender assesses their income, credit, and existing debts to decide whether and on what terms to lend.

notes.title: BorrowerDisclosureRequirement
notes.note: In the US these are implemented through the TILA-RESPA Integrated Disclosure (TRID) rule, giving borrowers standardized Loan Estimate and Closing Disclosure documents.

notes.title: BorrowerMonthlyIncome
notes.note: A key underwriting input; combined with monthly debt obligations it yields the debt-to-income ratio that drives the credit decision.

notes.title: ClosedEndMortgageLoan
notes.note: The common purchase or refinance mortgage: the full amount is advanced at closing and repaid on a fixed schedule, with no further draws permitted.

notes.title: Collateral
notes.note: For a mortgage the collateral is the financed real property; on default the lender can foreclose and sell it to recover the debt.

notes.title: CollateralValuation
notes.note: Typically an appraisal or automated valuation model (AVM); its result feeds the loan-to-value ratio and lien decisions.

notes.title: CollateralizedLoan
notes.note: A secured loan where specific assets (real property, securities, or cash) are pledged; the collateral reduces the lender's loss if the borrower defaults.

notes.title: ConsumerCreditProtectionLaw
notes.note: In the US this is the umbrella statute whose titles include the Truth in Lending Act (Regulation Z) and the Equal Credit Opportunity Act (Regulation B).

notes.title: CreditAgreement
notes.note: The binding contract — note plus loan and security agreement — setting the amount, rate, repayment schedule, and the lender's remedies on default.

notes.title: Creditor
notes.note: The party entitled to repayment; in a mortgage the lender is the creditor, though the debt may later be sold to another creditor in the secondary market.

notes.title: HMDA-CoveredLoanContract
notes.note: A loan or application subject to Home Mortgage Disclosure Act reporting; the lender must record specified data points about it on its register.

notes.title: HMDA-Report
notes.note: The Loan/Application Register (LAR) a covered institution files annually with the CFPB; regulators use it to detect discriminatory or risky lending patterns.

notes.title: IncomeVerificationReport
notes.note: Documents such as pay stubs, W-2s, tax returns, or a verification of employment that support claimed income and help satisfy ability-to-repay rules.

notes.title: IndividualPersonCreditRating
notes.note: Usually expressed as a credit score (e.g. FICO); a primary input to loan pricing and the approval decision.

notes.title: LegalObligation
notes.note: The borrower's court-enforceable duty to repay under the credit agreement; failure gives the lender legal remedies, including foreclosure of the collateral.

notes.title: Lender
notes.note: The party advancing funds; a lender may originate a loan to hold it, or to sell it into the secondary market while keeping or transferring servicing.

notes.title: LenderLienPosition
notes.note: First-lien loans are repaid before junior liens on default, so lien position strongly affects a loan's risk and its pricing.

notes.title: Loan
notes.note: The foundational debt instrument the other concepts specialize — money advanced now against a promise to repay according to agreed terms.

notes.title: LoanApplication
notes.note: Captures the borrower, the property, the terms sought, and financials (in the US, Form 1003); submitting it initiates underwriting.

notes.title: LoanApplicationPhase
notes.note: Tracks where an application sits in the workflow — for example pre-qualification, intake, underwriting, or decisioning — often driving permissions and turn-time targets.

notes.title: LoanApplicationStatus
notes.note: A point-in-time state (submitted, in review, approved, declined, withdrawn) used for pipeline reporting and borrower communication.

notes.title: LoanProductOffering
notes.note: A specific packaging of a loan product — its rate, term, and eligibility — marketed to a borrower segment or tailored to an individual applicant.

notes.title: LoanPhase
notes.note: The stage of a loan across its whole life (origination, disbursement, repayment, payoff), distinct from the application workflow.

notes.title: LoanProduct
notes.note: A standardized template — such as a 30-year fixed conventional, a 5/1 ARM, or an FHA loan — with defined characteristics that individual loans instantiate.

notes.title: LoanPurpose
notes.note: Why the credit is drawn (purchase, refinance, cash-out, or improvement); it affects eligibility, pricing, and the disclosures required.

notes.title: LoanToValueRatio
notes.note: A primary risk measure: a higher LTV means less borrower equity and greater loss on default, and above roughly 80% it commonly triggers mortgage insurance.

notes.title: MonetaryAmount
notes.note: A measured amount of money in a currency; the type used for principal, payments, income, and valuations throughout origination.

notes.title: MortgageLoanPurpose
notes.note: Specializes loan purpose for real-estate lending — purchase, rate-and-term refinance, cash-out refinance, or construction.

notes.title: MortgageProduct
notes.note: A loan product realized as a mortgage; conforming vs jumbo, fixed vs adjustable, and government-backed vs conventional are common distinctions.

notes.title: NMLSR-ID
notes.note: The NMLS identifier uniquely identifies licensed mortgage companies and loan originators; it appears on loan documents to support accountability.

notes.title: OpenEndMortgageLoan
notes.note: Permits additional advances up to a limit after closing (as with a home-equity line), unlike a closed-end mortgage.

notes.title: Payment
notes.note: A transfer of money settling part of the obligation; mortgage payments typically bundle principal, interest, and escrowed taxes and insurance.

notes.title: PaymentHistory
notes.note: The borrower's record of on-time and late payments; central to credit scoring and to servicing and default decisions.

notes.title: Principal
notes.note: The amount borrowed that must be repaid, separate from interest; the outstanding principal drives interest accrual and the payoff amount.

notes.title: PropertyUsage
notes.note: How the borrower will use the property — primary residence, second home, or investment — which affects the rate, LTV limits, and disclosures.

notes.title: RepaymentPhase
notes.note: Begins after disbursement; the borrower makes scheduled payments per the amortization schedule until the loan is paid off.

notes.title: SecuredLoan
notes.note: Backed by pledged assets or a guarantee, which lowers the lender's risk relative to an unsecured loan and usually the borrower's rate as well.

notes.title: TotalDebtExpenseRatio
notes.note: The debt-to-income (DTI) ratio; under US ability-to-repay / Qualified Mortgage rules it is commonly capped around 43%.

notes.title: TotalOutstandingPrincipal
notes.note: The aggregate unpaid principal secured by the property across all loans; used with appraised value to compute the combined loan-to-value ratio.

notes.title: UnderwritingDecision
notes.note: The outcome of assessing credit, capacity, and collateral — approve, approve with conditions, suspend, or deny.
